html:
Enersense International Oyj (ESENSE.HE)

Enersense is not a clean “3x earnings” stock today. That old bullish version was too aggressive. At €3.90, the company is a €63 million micro-cap with €18 million of Q1 net debt and a €29 million hybrid instrument that should be treated as debt-like for valuation discipline. But that correction does not kill the thesis. It makes it better defined: Enersense is a messy, under-followed Nordic infrastructure turnaround where 2026 is the proof year and 2027-2028 earnings power can make today’s equity look cheap.

The market still labels Enersense as a failed energy-transition contractor. That label is earned by history: volatile project margins, heavy restructuring, divestments, no dividend, Q1 2026 EPS of -€0.19, and ugly operating cash flow. The variant view is that the bad business mix has largely been carved away, while the remaining company is positioned directly in Finland’s and the Baltics’ grid, data center, substation, battery storage, district heating, and telecom buildout.

The current business is not a wind developer anymore. In 2025 Enersense sold its wind and solar project-development business to Fortum, exited or discontinued other non-core activities, and ended the year with 2025 revenue of €306.9 million, adjusted core EBITDA of €18.8 million, and a €392 million order book. Q1 2026 then lifted the order book to €413 million, with Power at a record €248 million. That Power backlog is roughly 1.8x the unit’s 2025 revenue, which gives real visibility if execution is competent.

The macro tailwind is unusually tangible. Fingrid’s updated plan calls for €5.2 billion of grid investment by 2035, including €2.0 billion in 2025-2028, and says the program can enable 10 GW of new industrial electricity demand and a doubling of consumption and production over the next decade. Microsoft also confirmed in April 2026 that phase two of its Finnish data center region will start construction in 2026. These are exactly the types of projects that need substations, transmission lines, power connections, fiber, heat recovery, and maintenance.

Enersense has already moved from narrative to order intake. It has a 125 MW / 250 MWh BESS delivery for OX2’s Kannisto wind farm, a Latvian full-scope BESS and 110 kV grid connection contract, data center fiber and power work near Riga, a >€30 million Helen operations and maintenance extension, and an Elektrilevi framework covering thousands of kilometers of lines and 1,800 substations. It also created a dedicated Data Centre Unit in April, explicitly targeting substations, power lines, data connections, and heat recovery.

The valuation is not screamingly cheap on 2026 net income, because 2026 still absorbs financing cost, working-capital normalization, and project timing. The company guides to €19-23 million of 2026 adjusted EBITDA. At today’s price, EV is about €81 million excluding the hybrid and about €111 million treating the hybrid as debt-like, so 2026 EV/EBITDA is roughly 3.5-5.8x depending on capital-structure treatment. That is cheap, but not absurd.

The real setup is 2027-2028. Enersense raised its 2025-2028 growth target to 6-7% CAGR and targets an EBITDA margin above 7%. On €375-400 million of revenue, that implies €26-28 million-plus EBITDA before any bull-case upside. Inderes’ post-Q1 estimate is more conservative, with 2028 revenue of €398 million, EBITDA margin of 6.6%, net income of €13.6 million, and EPS of €0.67. At €3.90, that is 5.8x 2028 earnings. If management actually reaches the >7% margin target, EPS could move toward €0.80-1.00.

There is also hidden asset torque. The Fortum transaction included a variable earn-out of up to €74 million, with Enersense previously estimating a probability-weighted €33 million. I would not underwrite the whole number, because timing depends on Fortum project FIDs and cash flows start no earlier than 2027, but even partial realization is large against a €63 million market cap.

The stock should work if three things happen: H2 2026 validates guidance, cash flow recovers after the Q1 VAT and working-capital hit, and Power converts backlog without margin leakage. The biggest risk is simple contractor risk. A low-margin project business can destroy equity value quickly if pricing, labor, permitting, or execution goes wrong. Q1 already showed permitting delays in two key Power projects. The balance sheet is better than in 2024, but not bulletproof once the hybrid is treated honestly.

My view: Enersense is a high-upside but execution-sensitive small-cap turnaround, not a sleep-well compounder. The market is still paying for a damaged contractor. The forward business is becoming a Nordic/Baltic grid and data center infrastructure services platform with visible demand and improving cost structure.

Bear case: €2.30, -41%. EBITDA misses guidance, cash flow stays weak, project delays persist, and the market values it as a levered contractor at a distressed discount to book.

Base case: €5.80, +49%. 2027 EBITDA reaches €23-25 million, free cash flow turns positive, net debt falls, and the stock trades around 8-9x normalized EPS with modest credit for the Fortum earn-out.

Bull case: €8.50, +118%. Data center and grid work accelerate, EBITDA margin approaches or exceeds 7%, EPS moves toward €0.90-1.00, and the market rerates Enersense from broken contractor to critical infrastructure lifecycle partner. Current stock price: €3.90.
